nuggets.headline: Daft Punk Crafted 'Homework' Working Just 8 Hours a Week
nuggets.text: Thomas Bangalter and Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo assembled the 16 tracks for _Homework_ over five months, working casually in Bangalter's bedroom. Their work ethic was deliberately relaxed—no more than eight hours a week—as they initially had no intention of making a full album, focusing instead on crafting standalone singles.
nuggets.imageCaption: Thomas Bangalter and Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo, circa the Homework era.

nuggets.headline: 'Around the World' Got Its Final Mix on an 11-Year-Old's Boombox
nuggets.text: After tracking the song's elements to DAT tape and looping them in samplers like the E-mu SP-1200, the final mixdown happened on a JVC boombox Thomas Bangalter had owned since he was 11. The duo credited this unconventional method for giving the track its distinctive lo-fi warmth, a character that defined the sound of the entire _Homework_ album. Listen for that slightly compressed, unified texture that glues the bass, drums, and synth together.
nuggets.imageCaption: Around the World by Daft Punk

nuggets.headline: For Another Take on French Retro-Futurism, Explore Kavinsky
nuggets.text: While "Around the World" recontextualizes '70s funk, fellow French producer Kavinsky applies a similar retro-futurist lens to '80s synth-noir and film scores. His work shares Daft Punk's affinity for analog gear and strong narrative concepts, creating cinematic electronic music that feels like a direct descendant of the scene _Homework_ helped build.
nuggets.imageCaption: French electronic artist Kavinsky, known for his '80s-inspired synthwave sound.

nuggets.headline: Daft Punk's Studio dynamic: No Ego, Just Instruments
nuggets.text: Inside Daft Punk's creative process, there was no fixed instrumental hierarchy. While Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo generally handled more guitar and Thomas Bangalter played more keyboards and bass, they both played all three instruments. Their philosophy was ego-less, with instrument duties assigned based purely on what the track needed, ensuring their focus remained on the final composition rather than individual roles.

nuggets.headline: This Bassline Is a Direct Homage to Nile Rodgers' Chic
nuggets.text: The driving, funky bassline you're hearing is Thomas Bangalter's direct attempt to create a Chic record using only the gear in his bedroom. He explicitly stated the goal was to channel Nile Rodgers' iconic funk style, but since they couldn't afford to collaborate with him, Bangalter played the bassline on a synthesizer and processed the vocals through a talk box to achieve that distinctive groove. It's a testament to their philosophy of recontextualizing past sounds with their available tools.
nuggets.imageCaption: Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo and Thomas Bangalter around the release of 'Homework'.

nuggets.headline: For More French Touch Bass, Listen to Bangalter's Stardust Collaborator Alan Braxe
nuggets.text: If the hypnotic, filtered bass of 'Around the World' is your target, go directly to Alan Braxe. He was Thomas Bangalter's partner in the one-off supergroup Stardust, and his solo work, particularly with Fred Falke, is foundational to the French Touch sound. Their track 'Intro' is pure gold—a masterclass in building a track around a relentlessly groovy, filtered bass loop that shares the exact same DNA as this Daft Punk classic.
nuggets.imageCaption: French DJ and producer Alan Braxe, a key figure in the French Touch scene.

nuggets.headline: Daft Punk's 'Homework' Cover Was a Week-Long DIY Fabric Project
nuggets.text: Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo designed the now-famous logo, and the duo had a precise vision for the album's physical presence. They spent a week with photographer Nicolas Hidiroglou testing different fabrics before landing on the final black satin for the cover. The inner sleeve is even more personal—it’s a photograph of Thomas Bangalter’s actual desk, where the album was made.
nuggets.imageCaption: The iconic Daft Punk logo embroidered on black satin for 'Homework'.

nuggets.headline: Each Dancer in the 'Around the World' Video Represents a Specific Instrument
nuggets.text: Director Michel Gondry and choreographer Blanca Li created a visual deconstruction of the track's layered composition. The song has five distinct instrumental parts, each personified by a group of dancers: robots for the talk box vocals, athletes for the bassline, synchronized swimmers for the synth, skeletons for the guitar, and mummies for the drum machine. Their synchronized movements bring the song's hypnotic, interlocking patterns to life.
nuggets.imageCaption: A still from the iconic Michel Gondry-directed 'Around the World' video.

nuggets.headline: For Daft Punk's Abrasive Techno Roots, Hear Their Teacher Neil Landstrumm
nuggets.text: While "Around the World" is pure filter-disco, 'Homework' also contains raw, punishing techno. Daft Punk listed Scottish producer Neil Landstrumm as one of their "Teachers," and the influence is undeniable—the distorted grind of their track 'Rollin’ & Scratchin’' bears a blatant similarity to Landstrumm’s 'Sniff And Destroy'. Landstrumm's raw, machine-funk is the aggressive flip-side to their pop sensibility.
nuggets.imageCaption: Scottish techno producer Neil Landstrumm, cited as a key influence by Daft Punk.
